title: Chapter 40: Terms of Employment
content: One week.
That's how long it takes to remember what your own life sounds like when it's not being measured in milestones.

I'm sitting in *my* apartment. Not Mephisto MCN's corporate housing with its floor-to-ceiling windows and its soulless, and I mean that literally, minimalist design. My place. The one I had before all of this. The one with the leaky faucet that drips like it's keeping time with some song only it can hear. The one that smells like fresh paint — I started painting again two days ago, and I am *terrible* at it, gloriously, catastrophically terrible. Turpentine fumes mixing with old radiator heat and it smells like *mine*.
The radiator kicks on with a groan like an old man asked to do one more thing before he retires.
And from the kitchen, I can hear Leo.
He's humming.
He's humming badly — off-key, sliding between notes like a car on black ice, no destination in mind.
I sit there on my couch — my couch, with the dip in the middle from years of one specific butt in one specific spot — and I listen to him hum, and I feel it.
Not hear it. Feel it.

The warmth of that sound. The way it fills the kitchen and spills into the living room. The way it fills the space where the fear used to live. Not replaces it — nothing replaces four hundred and sixteen thousand deleted souls. Nothing replaces what they lost. But the warmth is *there*, present and undeniable, like a hand on your back when you didn't know you needed one.
He is here. He is humming. He is making coffee in my kitchen.
And I can feel it.
God, I can feel *everything*.
The buzzer rings at eleven.

Evelyn's voice crackles through the intercom: "I brought bagels. And champagne. And something else. Buzz me up before the champagne gets warm and the bagels get cold, which I'm aware is a contradiction but I refuse to acknowledge it."
I buzz her in.

She arrives in weekend clothes — slouchy pants, a blazer that costs more than my rent, sunglasses pushed up on her head. Paper bag in one hand, bottle of Veuve in the other, flat envelope under her arm.
"Brunch," she says, like she's announcing a legal ruling. "Not a strategy session. Not a debrief. Brunch."
"Okay," I say.
"Don't 'okay' me like you're humoring me."
"I'm not humoring you. I'm genuinely excited about bagels."

She pushes past me into the apartment and stops. Takes in the paint-stained drop cloth, the canvases, the sketchbook on the coffee table, the half-finished painting on the easel — hot pink and black and gold, abstract, angry, alive.
"You're painting again," she says.
"I'm bad at it."
"Good." She says it firmly. Like a verdict. "Good."

She sets the bagels on the counter. Leo appears, kisses her on the cheek — she tolerates this with a slight jaw-tightening that means she's pleased — and starts plating. The champagne gets opened with the confidence of someone who's popped cork at a thousand closing dinners.

Evelyn pours three glasses. Hands me one. The bubbles hit my tongue — bright, yeasty, tasting like celebration and also like loss, because that's what champagne tastes like when you're drinking it for four hundred and thirty-one thousand people who got their emotions back and four hundred and sixteen thousand who didn't.
Then she pulls the envelope from under her arm. Opens it. Takes out a photograph.

It's the one from her office. Six-year-old me on my grandmother's lap — Grandma with her reading glasses perched on her nose and her hands around a cup of tea, looking down at me like I was the most interesting thing in any room. And behind us, Evelyn. Her hand on my shoulder. Not squeezing. Just *there*. Present in a way she didn't always know how to be.
She walks to the refrigerator. Finds a magnet — a plastic slice of pizza I bought from a museum gift shop in 2019 for three dollars, don't ask — and pins the photograph to the door.
I look at it.
And I feel everything.

All of it. At once. The grief — for my grandmother, for the 416K, for the Chloe who signed that contract without reading the fine print. The nostalgia — for a kitchen that smelled like tea and bread. The love — not just for them, for everyone. For the rescued. For the lost. For Leo and his terrible humming. For Evelyn and her hand that was always on my shoulder even when I couldn't feel it.
All of it. Mixed together. A chord that's finally, *finally* resolved.
"I'm not going to cry," I say.
Evelyn hands me a napkin without looking at me.
I cry.
Here's what happened to the company.

I renamed it. Mephisto MCN is dead — the name, the brand, the logo with its little pitchfork serif that I can't believe I never noticed. In its place: Hart Consulting Group. The most aggressively boring name I could come up with. It sounds like a firm that helps mid-size businesses optimize supply chains. It sounds like a place where nobody's soul gets extracted because everyone's too busy updating spreadsheets.
Good.

The app has been redesigned from the ground up. No emotional extraction. No milestone deductions. No demonic jurisdiction clause hidden in section 47.3 of the Terms of Service like a land mine at a picnic. It's a content management platform now. Users upload, store, and share their work. That's it. Boring. Legal. *Human*.

The 431,000 archived emotions that survived — every single one released. I pushed the button myself. Watched the counter tick to zero. Watched the data packets route back to their owners like homing pigeons after a storm. I don't know what it felt like on the other end — four hundred and thirty-one thousand people suddenly feeling something they'd forgotten they'd lost. I hope it was like waking up. I hope it was warm.
The 416,000 that were deleted — the ones that froze at 49.3% — I can't bring them back. Deletion is deletion, even in Hell.
But I can do something.

The Hollowed Foundation. Named after what they were — hollowed out, emptied, used up. Mental health services, legal aid, career support for former creators. Free. Forever. Funded by the company's existing assets, which — and I will never not find this darkly funny — are substantial. Harvesting human emotions turns out to be extremely profitable.
I can't give them back what they lost.
But I can make sure nobody ever loses it again.
Leo sits down next to me on the couch. He has his laptop open, the screen a cascade of code so dense it might as well be ancient Greek. Which, given the context, feels on the nose.
"I want to show you something," he says.
"Is it going to make me cry? Because Evelyn already has that covered."

"Possibly." He turns the laptop toward me. "I built a safeguard. A watchdog program. It monitors the Hart Consulting Group servers for any sign of emotional data extraction — any pattern, any anomaly, any attempt to harvest emotions from users. If it detects even a hint of it, the system shuts down automatically and alerts the FTC."

I look at the code. I don't understand it. Like, *at all*. The variable names could be ingredients for all I know. There are brackets inside brackets inside brackets, like Russian dolls made of syntax.

"It's a dead man's switch for the soul," he says. He starts explaining the technical details — the detection thresholds, the failover protocols, the automated reporting pipeline, the Byzantine fault tolerance, and I understand maybe one word in five. But I listen.
Not because I understand the code.

Because his voice, when he talks about his work, is one of my favorite sounds. It's careful and precise and a little bit excited, the way someone gets when they're telling you about the thing they were put on earth to do. He gestures with his free hand. His eyes get bright. He's *here*, fully present, building something that protects people, and the love I feel for him in this moment is so enormous and so specific that it makes my chest hurt.
I reach over and take his hand.
He stops talking.
He looks at our hands. His fingers are warm. A little paint-stained — he helped me with the drop cloth. The nails are chewed short. Developer hands. My hands.
He looks at me.
I smile. "Keep talking. I like the sound."
He resumes — failover clusters, redundant backup, something about container orchestration. His hand stays in mine. His voice wraps around me like a frequency I finally know how to tune into.
I think: *this is what it feels like to be protected by someone who chooses it.*
Not a contract. Not a clause. A hand.
The doorbell rings.
I look at Leo. He shrugs. Evelyn frowns.
I get up and walk to the door. Look through the peephole.
And there, standing in my hallway — *my* hallway, with its cracked tile and its buzzing fluorescent that the super keeps promising to fix — is Mephisto.
He's not in a suit.
Let me say that again, because it deserves the emphasis: He. Is. Not. In. A. Suit.

Dark jeans. A black sweater. No tie. No pocket square. No watch that costs more than a car. He looks almost... diminished. Smaller. Like a CEO who's been told the board voted him out, which is exactly what happened — the board was me and the vote was unanimous.
He looks at the peephole. He knows I'm looking. Of course he does.
"May I come in?" he says.
I consider.

I should be afraid. I can feel the fear — it's there, a tremor in my chest, light and fast, like a bird trapped in a room. But I also feel curiosity. And something else. Something harder to name. Not forgiveness — not yet, maybe not ever. But *acknowledgment*. He's my adversary. He's the architect of every nightmare I've had for the past year. He's also the reason I became who I am. The pressure that made the diamond. The fire that tempered the steel. Pick your metaphor — they're all true, and they all make me want to throw something at his head.
I open the door wider.
"Five minutes."

He sits on my couch. *My* couch. In *my* apartment. And I watch him look around — the sketchbook on the table, the photograph on the refrigerator (he sees it; something shifts in his expression, too fast to read), the painting on the easel, hot pink and black and gold, and I don't know what he thinks of it and I don't care.
The power dynamic has completely flipped, and we both know it. He's in my space now. My terms. My rooms. My rules.
He looks at the painting for a long moment. Then he turns to me.

"I've been thinking about what you said," he says. "About being my own worst enemy." He pauses. He's not performing this time — I've spent enough time watching him perform to know the difference. This is something else. Something quieter. "You were right. I built the system that built you. Every clause, every deduction, every milestone — they sharpened you into something I couldn't predict. A blade that cut the handle off."
He looks at his hands. No rings. No cufflinks. Empty hands.
"I'd like to work for you."
I stare at him.
"Excuse me?"

He smiles. And it's not the CEO smile — the one that means he's already won. It's not the demon smile — the one that means you should read the contract three times before you sign. It's something I've never seen on his face. Something weary and ancient and almost human. The smile of someone who has been doing the same job for millennia and is finally, *finally* exhausted by it.

"You need someone who understands infernal architecture," he says. "The system is still running, Ms. Hart. Hell doesn't close because you changed the signage. The pipes, the servers, the extraction protocols — they're all still there. Dormant, but present. You need a consultant who knows the code." He meets my eyes. "And I need... employment."
I look at him for a long time.

The fear is still there. The tremor. The part of me that wants to slam the door and bolt it and never see his face again. But there's another part — the part that learned to read contracts, to find the hidden clauses, to understand that the person holding the pen has the power. And that part says: *Use every tool you have. Even the ones that cut you. Especially those.*

I pick up the pen from the coffee table. The metal is cold against my fingers. I grab the notepad next to it — the one with the grocery list on the previous page (eggs, milk, turpentine, more paint, Leo's preferred coffee beans).
I write, in careful capital letters:
**TERMS OF EMPLOYMENT**
I look up at him.

"No emotional extraction. No data harvesting. No jurisdiction games. You work for *human* law. You answer to *me*. And if you so much as *look* at someone's soul sideways, I will revoke your access and report you to every three-letter agency I can think of."
I slide the notepad across the coffee table.
"Sign at the bottom."

He picks up the pen. He reads the terms — all twelve words of them. His mouth twitches. Something that might be respect. Something that might be amusement. Something that is definitely, unmistakably, *pride* — not in himself, but in me. In what his system built.
He signs.
And for the first time in millennia, a demon signs a contract he didn't write.

The signature is elegant. Archaic. Loops and flourishes that belong in a museum, not on a notepad from a bodega. It's binding — I know that the way I know gravity works, the way I know fire burns. Infernal signatures don't need witnesses. They ARE the witness.
He stands. Straightens his sweater. Walks to the door.
He pauses.
"You know," he says, not turning around, "in all my years of dealing, I've never met a human who read the fine print."
I say: "I didn't read it the first time. That's why I learned to write it."
A beat. The smallest nod. He leaves.
The door closes, and the sound of it is the full stop at the end of a sentence that's been running since day one.
From the kitchen, Leo's voice: "Did you just hire a demon?"
"I just gave one a job."

I look at the notepad. His signature sits in the margin — elegant, archaic, and binding. A demon's word, on my terms, in my apartment, on a notepad that still has "eggs, milk, turpentine" written on the back.
I tear off the page. Walk to the refrigerator. Put it up next to the photograph.
Two contracts. One took everything. One gave it back.

I stand there, looking at them side by side. The photograph — me at six, held by people who loved me in their imperfect, human, fumbling way. The signature — a demon's word, bound to my terms, on my refrigerator, held by a plastic slice of pizza from a museum gift shop.
I turn to Leo. He's leaning against the kitchen counter, coffee mug in hand, watching me with those dark eyes that see everything and never flinch.
I turn to Evelyn. She's sitting at my table, refilling her champagne glass, not crying, absolutely not crying, just cutting a bagel with extreme precision so she has something to do with her hands.

I look at my apartment. My painting — hot pink and black and gold, terrible and mine. My photo on my fridge. My life, with its leaky faucet and its cracked tiles and its radiator that sounds like it's arguing with itself.
I am Chloe Hart.
I have empathy — the real kind, the kind that hurts, the kind I earned back one deleted soul at a time.
I have trust — not the naive kind, not the contract kind. The kind you build by watching someone hum off-key in your kitchen at seven in the morning.
I have grief — for the 416,000. I will carry them. Always. That's the deal I made with myself, and it's the only contract that matters.

I have nostalgia — not for a past that was simpler, but for the feeling of being small and held and warm, which I now know I can build for myself, on my own terms, in my own apartment, with my own terrible painting on the wall.
I have love. Messy, imperfect, off-key love. The kind that stays.
I read the terms.
And I rewrote them.
The new terms are mine.
**THE END**
